activities_projects.title: Defining the Pastoralist Life
activities_projects.objective: Understand the basic lifestyle of people who move with herds
steps: Define the term 'pastoralist' based on your reading.
List three animals that pastoralists typically herd (e.g., sheep, goats, camels).
Explain in one sentence why these groups move from one place to another.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes pastoralism as a way of life based on animal herding and movement.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clear definition and correct listing of animals (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Mountains and Movements
activities_projects.objective: Identify pastoral communities of the Himalayas
steps: Identify the Gujjar Bakarwals of Jammu and Kashmir.
Describe their movement: Where do they go in winter and where do they go in summer?
Draw a simple mountain to show the high 'bugyals' (vast meadows) they visit in summer.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the concept of seasonal migration between high and low altitudes.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate description of seasonal cycles (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Mapping the Pastoral Groups of India
activities_projects.objective: Locate major pastoral communities on the map of India
steps: Find the regions for the following groups: Gaddi shepherds (Himachal Pradesh), Raikas (Rajasthan), and Dhangars (Maharashtra).
Color these states on your map using different colors.
Label each state with the name of its corresponding pastoral group.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes the geographic distribution of pastoralism across India.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct mapping and labeling of the three groups (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Impact of Colonial Laws
activities_projects.objective: Identify how British rule changed pastoral lives
steps: Identify the 'Waste Land Rules' and explain why the British considered grazing land as 'waste'.
List one way the Forest Acts affected pastoralists (e.g., restricted entry into forests).
Define the 'Criminal Tribes Act' and note which groups were targeted.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the negative impact of colonial legislation on traditional grazing rights.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate identification of colonial acts and their effects (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Pastoralism in Africa: The Maasai
activities_projects.objective: Learn about pastoral communities outside of India
steps: Identify the Maasai community and the continent they live in (Africa).
Find out what the name 'Maasai' means ('My People').
List two modern-day countries where the Maasai live (Kenya and Tanzania).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Appreciates the global presence of pastoral communities.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of geography and meanings (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Life in the Plateau: The Dhangars
activities_projects.objective: Study the interaction between herders and farmers
steps: Identify the Dhangars of Maharashtra.
Explain the relationship between the Dhangar shepherds and the Konkani peasants.
Note what the shepherds received from the farmers (e.g., rice) in exchange for manuring the fields.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the mutual benefit between pastoralists and settled farmers.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clear explanation of the Dhangar-Konkani interaction (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Raikas of the Desert
activities_projects.objective: Understand how pastoralism adapts to dry climates
steps: Identify the Raikas of Rajasthan.
Explain why they combine cultivation with pastoralism.
Sketch a camel to represent the Maru Raikas who herd camels in the Thar desert.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes adaptation strategies in arid environments.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate description of Raika lifestyle and adaptation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Closing the Borders
activities_projects.objective: Understand how international boundaries affect movement
steps: Describe how the partition of India and Pakistan in 1947 affected the Raikas.
Identify why they can no longer graze their camels on the banks of the Indus river.
Name the new state they now travel to (e.g., Haryana) for grazing.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Realizes how modern political borders disrupt traditional migratory paths.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of the effects of Partition (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Maasai Mara and Conservation
activities_projects.objective: Identify the conflict between wildlife parks and grazing
steps: List two famous game reserves in Africa (e.g., Maasai Mara, Serengeti).
Explain what happened to the pastoralists when these areas were turned into parks.
Color a small section of a page to represent a 'Game Reserve' where grazing is banned.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the tension between modern conservation and traditional livelihoods.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clear explanation of the impact of game reserves (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Adapting to Change
activities_projects.objective: Understand how pastoralists survive in the modern world
steps: Identify two ways pastoralists have adapted to new laws (e.g., changing grazing grounds, reducing herd size).
Mention how some rich pastoralists have settled down and started trading.
Write a concluding sentence on why pastoralism is still a relevant way of life today.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the resilience and adaptability of pastoral communities.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate description of modern adaptation strategies (10 marks)
